subject: Kubernetes
description: Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.

questions.question: What is Kubernetes?
questions.answer: Kubernetes (K8s) is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. Originally developed by Google and later donated to CNCF.
wrongAnswers: A Docker container runtime for managing local applications
A version control system for infrastructure code
A cloud provider managed database service

questions.question: Explain Kubernetes Architecture.
questions.answer: Kubernetes follows a master-worker architecture with Control Plane (API Server, etcd, Scheduler, Controller Manager) and Data Plane (Kubelet, Kube-proxy, Container Runtime).
wrongAnswers: A single-tier architecture where all components run on one node
A client-server architecture without any control plane
A serverless architecture using functions as pods

questions.question: What is Container Orchestration?
questions.answer: Container orchestration is the automated process of managing the lifecycle of software containers including provisioning, scaling, networking, load balancing, health monitoring, and self-healing.
wrongAnswers: The process of building Docker container images
A method for securing containers with TLS certificates
A technique for compressing container images

questions.question: What is a Pod in Kubernetes?
questions.answer: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes that represents one or more containers running in a shared environment, sharing networking and storage resources.
wrongAnswers: A single container without any shared resources
A Kubernetes cluster with multiple nodes
A virtual machine running inside Kubernetes

questions.question: What is the difference between a Pod and a container?
questions.answer: A container is a single isolated process with its own filesystem, while a Pod is a logical wrapper containing one or more tightly coupled containers that share network namespace, storage, and lifecycle.
wrongAnswers: There is no difference, they are the same thing
Pods run on VMs while containers run on bare metal
Containers are immutable while Pods are mutable

questions.question: What is the purpose of kubectl?
questions.answer: Kubectl is the primary CLI tool for managing Kubernetes resources and interacting with the cluster, used for commands like kubectl get, kubectl apply, kubectl describe, and kubectl exec.
wrongAnswers: A GUI tool for visualizing Kubernetes architecture
A configuration file format for Kubernetes manifests
A container runtime for executing pods

questions.question: What is a Deployment in Kubernetes?
questions.answer: A Deployment manages the lifecycle of Pods ensuring the desired number of replicas are running, supporting declarative updates, rolling updates, rollbacks, and self-healing.
wrongAnswers: A method for deploying databases in Kubernetes
A configuration file that describes only network settings
A tool for monitoring pod resource usage

questions.question: What is a Kubernetes Service and why is it needed?
questions.answer: A Service exposes a group of Pods and provides a stable network endpoint because Pods are ephemeral and their IPs change, requiring services for stable networking and load balancing.
wrongAnswers: A load balancer for external cloud resources only
A database connection pool for stateful applications
A firewall rule for securing cluster traffic

questions.question: What service types are available in Kubernetes?
questions.answer: Kubernetes provides four service types: ClusterIP (internal only), NodePort (exposes on static port), LoadBalancer (provisions external LB), and ExternalName (maps to external DNS).
wrongAnswers: Only ClusterIP and NodePort
PublicIP and PrivateIP
Internal, External, and Hybrid

questions.question: What are the key differences between ClusterIP, NodePort, and LoadBalancer?
questions.answer: ClusterIP provides internal-only access, NodePort exposes on static ports (30000-32767) on all nodes, and LoadBalancer provisions cloud provider load balancers for external traffic.
wrongAnswers: They are all the same with different names
ClusterIP is for external traffic, LoadBalancer is internal
NodePort is the most secure, LoadBalancer is deprecated

questions.question: What is the role of ConfigMaps and Secrets?
questions.answer: ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs, while Secrets store sensitive data like passwords and tokens with base64 encoding (though not encrypted by default).
wrongAnswers: Both store data in encrypted format by default
ConfigMaps are for secrets, Secrets are for configuration
They are identical except for the name

questions.question: What are Namespaces in Kubernetes?
questions.answer: Namespaces are virtual clusters within a Kubernetes cluster that provide isolation, help avoid naming conflicts, enable access control via RBAC, and support resource quotas.
wrongAnswers: Physical clusters on separate hardware
A way to run multiple Kubernetes clusters on one machine
A security feature that encrypts all cluster data

questions.question: What are Labels and Selectors in Kubernetes?
questions.answer: Labels are key/value pairs attached to objects for identification and organization, while Selectors filter resources based on Labels using equality-based or set-based operations.
wrongAnswers: Labels encrypt data, Selectors decrypt it
Labels are for pods only, Selectors work only with services
They are the same thing with different names

questions.question: What are Persistent Volumes (PVs) and PersistentVolumeClaims (PVCs)?
questions.answer: PV is a cluster-wide storage resource provisioned by admin or dynamically via StorageClass. PVC is a user's request for storage that claims specific size and access mode, binding to matching PV.
wrongAnswers: PVs are temporary storage, PVCs are permanent
PVs are for pods only, PVCs are for containers
They are the same thing with different names

questions.question: What is the role of etcd in Kubernetes?
questions.answer: etcd is a distributed, consistent, key-value store that serves as the central database for Kubernetes, storing desired state, current state, and cluster configuration.
wrongAnswers: A container runtime for executing pods
A load balancer for external traffic
A monitoring tool for cluster metrics

questions.question: What is the Kubernetes API Server?
questions.answer: The API Server is the front-end for Kubernetes control plane, serving as entry point for all REST operations, validating requests, and updating cluster state in etcd.
wrongAnswers: A database for storing container images
A scheduler for pod placement
A network proxy for pod communication

questions.question: What is the role of kube-scheduler?
questions.answer: The scheduler assigns newly created Pods to nodes based on filtering (predicates) and scoring (priorities) phases, then binds the Pod to the highest-scoring node.
wrongAnswers: It monitors pod health and restarts failed pods
It manages network communication between pods
It handles storage allocation for persistent volumes

questions.question: How does Kubernetes handle container scaling?
questions.answer: Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA) automatically scales Pod replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics by calculating and updating the replica count.
wrongAnswers: Through manual kubectl scale commands only
By adding more nodes to the cluster automatically
Through vertical scaling of pod resource limits

questions.question: How does Kubernetes autoscaling work?
questions.answer: Kubernetes provides three types of autoscaling: HPA adjusts pod count, VPA adjusts pod resource requests, and Cluster Autoscaler adjusts node count.
wrongAnswers: Only through manual kubectl commands
Through adding more replicas to deployments
Through vertical scaling of containers only

questions.question: What is the difference between HPA and VPA?
questions.answer: HPA adjusts the number of Pod replicas (horizontal scaling), while VPA adjusts CPU and memory requests for existing Pods (vertical scaling).
wrongAnswers: HPA is for horizontal scaling only, VPA is deprecated
They are the same thing with different names
HPA scales nodes, VPA scales pods

questions.question: What are Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes?
questions.answer: Liveness checks if container should be restarted, Readiness checks if container can serve traffic, Startup checks if application has started successfully.
wrongAnswers: All three are the same thing
Liveness checks CPU, Readiness checks memory
Startup replaces liveness and readiness probes

questions.question: What is a Sidecar Container?
questions.answer: A sidecar is a helper container running alongside the main application in the same Pod, used for logging, metrics, proxies, and configuration handling.
wrongAnswers: A container that replaces the main application
A container that runs in a separate pod
A container that scales independently from the main app

questions.question: What is the difference between Deployment, StatefulSet, and DaemonSet?
questions.answer: Deployment is for stateless apps, StatefulSet is for stateful apps with stable identity, and DaemonSet runs one pod on each node.
wrongAnswers: They are all the same with different names
Deployment is deprecated, use StatefulSet only
DaemonSet is for stateful apps only

questions.question: What are init containers and when would you use them?
questions.answer: Init containers run and complete BEFORE app containers start, used for waiting on dependencies, setup tasks, or migrations.
wrongAnswers: Containers that initialize the cluster
Containers that restart when apps fail
Containers that run in parallel with the main app

questions.question: What is a Kubernetes Ingress and how does it work?
questions.answer: Ingress manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to Services with URL-based routing, SSL termination, and load balancing features.
wrongAnswers: A load balancer for TCP/UDP traffic only
A network firewall for Kubernetes
A service mesh configuration tool

questions.question: What is Ingress Default Backend?
questions.answer: The Ingress Default Backend specifies what to do with requests that don't match any routing rules, providing a fallback for misconfigured routes.
wrongAnswers: A backend that handles all traffic by default
A database connection pool for Ingress
A TLS termination proxy

questions.question: How should TLS be configured with Ingress?
questions.answer: TLS should be configured in the Ingress spec using a TLS secret containing the certificate and private key, with hosts specified in both tls and rules sections.
wrongAnswers: TLS is automatically configured by Kubernetes
TLS requires a separate LoadBalancer service
TLS can only be configured at the node level

questions.question: What is the Kubernetes Gateway API?
questions.answer: The Gateway API is the modern evolution of Kubernetes networking with role-oriented design separating Gateway infrastructure from Routes.
wrongAnswers: A legacy API for managing cluster gateways
A replacement for all Kubernetes APIs
A network security plugin only

questions.question: What are Taints and Tolerations?
questions.answer: Taints repel Pods from nodes unless they have matching tolerations, used to reserve nodes for specific workloads.
wrongAnswers: Taints attract Pods, tolerations repel them
Both are the same thing
Taints are for storage, tolerations are for networking

questions.question: What are Affinity and Anti-Affinity rules?
questions.answer: Affinity attracts Pods to nodes or other Pods, while Anti-Affinity repels Pods, used for co-location or high availability.
wrongAnswers: Affinity is for storage, Anti-Affinity is for networking
They are the same as Taints and Tolerations
Affinity only works with nodes, not pods

questions.question: How do you run a Pod on a particular node?
questions.answer: You can use nodeName, nodeSelector, or Node Affinity to constrain a Pod to run on a specific node.
wrongAnswers: By manually moving the pod file to the node
By editing the node configuration directly
By creating a dedicated deployment for each node

questions.question: How does rolling updates work in a Deployment?
questions.answer: Rolling updates gradually replace old Pods with new ones by creating a new ReplicaSet and scaling them up while scaling down the old one.
wrongAnswers: It deletes all old pods first then creates new ones
It runs old and new pods in parallel indefinitely
It requires manual approval for each pod update

questions.question: What is the difference between Rolling Update and Recreate strategies?
questions.answer: Rolling Update replaces pods gradually with zero downtime, while Recreate terminates all pods first causing downtime.
wrongAnswers: They are identical strategies
Recreate has zero downtime
Rolling Update is faster than Recreate

questions.question: What is the difference between Deployments and ReplicaSets?
questions.answer: Deployment manages ReplicaSets declaratively with rolling updates and rollbacks, while ReplicaSet is the basic replication mechanism that ensures pod count.
wrongAnswers: They are the same thing
ReplicaSet is newer than Deployment
Deployment is deprecated, use ReplicaSet only

questions.question: What is the relationship between Deployment, ReplicaSet, and Pod?
questions.answer: Deployment creates and manages ReplicaSets, which creates and manages Pods in a hierarchy.
wrongAnswers: They are all independent resources
Pod creates Deployment which creates ReplicaSet
ReplicaSet is not related to Deployment or Pod

questions.question: What is a ReplicaSet and how does it ensure high availability?
questions.answer: ReplicaSet ensures specified number of Pod replicas are running by monitoring health and creating/deleting pods to maintain desired count.
wrongAnswers: It creates new nodes for pods
It automatically scales pods up and down
It's a deprecated resource

questions.question: What are Resource Requests and Limits?
questions.answer: Requests guarantee minimum resources for scheduling, while limits cap maximum resources a container can use.
wrongAnswers: Requests are optional, limits are required
They are the same thing
Limits are for pods, requests are for containers only

questions.question: What happens if a Pod exceeds its resource limits?
questions.answer: Memory limit exceeded causes OOM kill, while CPU limit exceeded causes throttling (not killed).
wrongAnswers: Container is automatically scaled up
Nothing happens, limits are just suggestions
Pod is migrated to another node

questions.question: How do you control resource usage of Pods?
questions.answer: Using requests and limits for CPU/memory, and LimitRange to set defaults per namespace.
wrongAnswers: Through manual configuration only
By limiting the number of nodes
Through RBAC policies only

questions.question: How does Kubernetes handle node failures?
questions.answer: Kubelet detects failures and reports to API server, then pods are evicted and rescheduled to healthy nodes based on PodDisruptionBudgets.
wrongAnswers: It automatically repairs the failed node
It deletes the node from the cluster
It restarts all pods on the failed node

questions.question: How do you do maintenance on a Kubernetes node?
questions.answer: Using kubectl cordon to disable scheduling, then kubectl drain to safely evict pods before maintenance.
wrongAnswers: By deleting and recreating the node
By restarting the kubelet service only
Through the Kubernetes Dashboard UI only

questions.question: What is a Pod Disruption Budget (PDB)?
questions.answer: PDB ensures minimum number of Pods available during voluntary disruptions like node draining.
wrongAnswers: A budget for storage resources
A limit on pod resource usage
A database connection pool configuration

questions.question: How do you troubleshoot a Pod stuck in Pending state?
questions.answer: By using kubectl describe to check events, which show reasons like insufficient resources, taints, or PVC issues.
wrongAnswers: By deleting and recreating the pod
By increasing the pod replica count
By restarting the cluster

questions.question: How do you troubleshoot a Pod in CrashLoopBackOff state?
questions.answer: By checking logs with kubectl logs --previous to see the actual error causing the crashes.
wrongAnswers: By increasing memory limits
By scaling up the deployment
By restarting the node

questions.question: How do you troubleshoot ImagePullBackOff?
questions.answer: By verifying image name, registry accessibility, and imagePullSecret configuration for private registries.
wrongAnswers: By increasing pod CPU limits
By using a different container runtime
By disabling image verification

questions.question: How do you get central logs from Pods?
questions.answer: Using DaemonSet log collectors like Fluentd, sidecar containers, or direct export to logging systems.
wrongAnswers: By manually accessing each pod shell
By enabling debug mode only
Through node-level debugging only

questions.question: How do you monitor a Kubernetes cluster?
questions.answer: Using Prometheus + Grafana with kube-state-metrics, node-exporter, and cAdvisor exporters.
wrongAnswers: Through manual inspection only
By checking pod logs daily
Using Docker stats only

questions.question: How do you implement zero-downtime Deployments?
questions.answer: Using rolling updates with readiness probes and PodDisruptionBudgets to ensure traffic isn't interrupted.
wrongAnswers: By deleting pods manually during updates
By scaling to zero then deploying
Through batch deployment only

questions.question: How do you perform a rolling restart of a Deployment?
questions.answer:
Methods for rolling restart:

1. kubectl rollout restart (recommended):
   kubectl rollout restart deployment/<name>

2. Scale to 0 and back:
   kubectl scale deployment/<name> --replicas=0
   kubectl scale deployment/<name> --replicas=<original>

3. Annotate to force restart:
   kubectl annotate deployment/<name> \
     kubernetes.io/change-cause="manual restart"
   kubectl patch deployment/<name> \
     -p '{"spec":{"template":{"metadata":{"annotations":{"date":"'$(date +'%s')"'}}}}}'

Monitor progress:
   kubectl rollout status deployment/<name>
wrongAnswers: By deleting all pods and letting them respawn automatically
By restarting the kubelet service on all nodes
Through the Kubernetes Dashboard only

questions.question: What are the different container runtime options?
questions.answer:
Container Runtimes:

containerd (default in most managed K8s):
- CNCF project
- Simple, stable
- Industry standard

CRI-O:
- Kubernetes-focused
- OCI-compliant
- Lightweight

Docker (legacy):
- Was the original runtime
- Uses containerd via dockershim
- Deprecated in K8s 1.24+

Mirantis Container Runtime:
- Docker-compatible
- Long-term Docker support

All use OCI (Open Container Initiative) specs for images and runtimes.
wrongAnswers: Rocket (rkt) and LXC containers only
Only Docker is supported in Kubernetes
Kata Containers and gVisor as the only options

questions.question: What is the role of kube-proxy?
questions.answer:
kube-proxy maintains network rules on nodes to enable Pod communication.

Modes:

1. iptables (default):
   - Programs iptables rules
   - Random load balancing
   - Less performant at scale

2. IPVS (IP Virtual Server):
   - L4 load balancing in kernel
   - Better performance at scale
   - More algorithms (rr, lc, sh, etc.)


3. userspace (legacy):
   - Proxy in user space
   - Higher latency
   - Not recommended

Function:
- Watches API server for Service changes
- Updates network rules accordingly
- Enables Pod-to-Pod and Service communication
wrongAnswers: Manages container images across the cluster
Handles DNS resolution for Services
Schedules Pods to worker nodes

questions.question: What are the various K8s services running on nodes?
questions.answer:
Worker Node Services:

1. Kubelet:
   - Agent running on each node
   - Ensures containers are running as specified
   - Reports node status to master
   - Only manages containers created by Kubernetes

2. Kube-proxy:
   - Maintains network rules
   - Handles Pod communication
   - Programs iptables or IPVS

3. Container Runtime:
   - Runs containers (containerd, CRI-O)
   - Pulls images
   - Creates containers

Control Plane Services:
1. Kube-apiserver: API entry point
2. Kube-scheduler: Assigns Pods to nodes
3. Kube-controller-manager: Runs controllers
4. etcd: Distributed database
wrongAnswers: Docker Engine and Harbor registry only
Only kubelet runs on worker nodes
kubectl and helm package manager

questions.question: What is CoreDNS and how does it work?
questions.answer:
CoreDNS is the default DNS service in Kubernetes clusters.

Function:
- Service discovery
- Resolves Service names to IPs
- DNS format: <service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local

How it works:
1. Pod needs to reach a Service
2. Pod queries kubelet DNS
3. kubelet forwards to cluster DNS (CoreDNS)
4. CoreDNS looks up Service in etcd
5. Returns ClusterIP

Features:
- Automatic updates when Services change
- Caching for performance
- Custom DNS configurations
- Configured via ConfigMap in kube-system
wrongAnswers: A load balancer for external traffic
A container image registry for the cluster
An authentication service for RBAC

questions.question: What is a Network Policy?
questions.answer:
NetworkPolicy defines how Pods communicate with each other and external endpoints.

By default, all Pods can talk to all Pods.

Example:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: api-allow-web
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: web

Key points:
- Acts as firewall at Layer 3/4
- Requires CNI plugin supporting policies
- Default deny all is best practice
wrongAnswers: A policy for limiting resource usage like CPU and memory
A configuration for storage volumes and persistent claims
A rule for scheduling pods based on node labels

questions.question: How does Kubernetes handle communication between Pods?
questions.answer:
Kubernetes networking allows communication between Pods, Services, and external clients.

Pod-to-Pod communication:
- Each Pod gets unique IP
- Pods can communicate within cluster using IPs
- Overlay network (CNI plugin) handles routing

Service-to-Pod communication:
- Services provide stable IP (ClusterIP)
- kube-proxy routes traffic to Pods
- Load balancing across Pods

External access:
- NodePort: Exposes on node port
- LoadBalancer: Cloud provider LB
- Ingress: HTTP/HTTPS routing

DNS:
- CoreDNS resolves Service names
- Format: <svc>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
wrongAnswers: Through direct host networking without any proxy
Using SSH tunnels between nodes
Via a centralized message queue service

questions.question: How can Pods in different Nodes communicate?
questions.answer:
Pods can communicate across nodes by default using the overlay network:

How it works:
1. Container runtime creates network namespace
2. CNI plugin (Calico, Flannel, Cilium) creates overlay
3. Virtual network assigns Pod IPs
4. CNI routes packets between nodes

Key concepts:
- Pod IP is not tied to node IP
- CNI handles cross-node networking
- No NAT required for Pod-to-Pod

Different CNI plugins have different implementations:
- Flannel: VXLAN encapsulation
- Calico: BGP routing or encapsulation
- Cilium: eBPF-based networking
wrongAnswers: Through port forwarding via the API server
Using external load balancers only
By configuring host ports in the Pod spec

questions.question: What is the difference between Docker Swarm and Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
Kubernetes:
- Complex, full-featured orchestration
- Steeper learning curve
- Large ecosystem and community
- Auto-scaling built-in
- Rolling updates and self-healing
- Best for: Production, complex microservices

Docker Swarm:
- Simpler, built into Docker
- Easier to learn and setup
- Smaller feature set
- Native Docker CLI integration
- Best for: Simple workloads, Docker-centric teams

Key differences:
| Feature | Kubernetes | Swarm |
|---------|------------|-------|
| Complexity | High | Low |
| Scaling | HPA built-in | Manual |
| Networking | CNI plugins | Overlay |
| Updates | Declarative | Built-in |
wrongAnswers: Swarm requires a separate installation unlike Kubernetes
Docker Swarm cannot run containers
Kubernetes is built into Docker Engine

questions.question: What is a Job and how does it differ from a CronJob?
questions.answer:
Job:
- Runs one or more Pods to completion
- Tracks successful completion count
- Retries failed Pods
- Use for: batch processing, migrations, one-time tasks

CronJob:
- Schedule Jobs to run at specific times
- Uses cron syntax: minute hour day month weekday
- Supports concurrency policy
- Use for: backups, reports, cleanup, scheduled tasks

Example CronJob:
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"  # Daily at 2 AM
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: backup-tool
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
wrongAnswers: Jobs run continuously while CronJobs run once
CronJobs cannot run shell commands
Jobs and CronJobs are the same resource type

questions.question: What is the purpose of ServiceAccount?
questions.answer:
ServiceAccount provides identity for Pods to access the Kubernetes API.

Default: default SA in each namespace
Custom: create for specific applications

Use cases:
- Pod authentication to API server
- Image pull secrets (robot accounts)
- RBAC permissions for applications

How it works:
1. Pod uses ServiceAccount
2. ServiceAccount has associated token
3. Token mounted as volume
4. API requests include token
5. RBAC validates permissions

Best practice: Use separate SA per application with minimal permissions.

Example:
spec:
  serviceAccountName: my-app-sa
wrongAnswers: A way to expose services externally
A database account for stateful applications
An authentication method for users logging into the cluster

questions.question: What is Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
RBAC controls who can do what in the cluster.

Components:
- Role/ClusterRole: Define permissions
- RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding: Assign permissions to subjects

Example Role:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

Example RoleBinding:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: read-pods
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: jane
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: pod-reader

Scope:
- Role: Namespace-scoped
- ClusterRole: Cluster-wide or can be bound to namespace
wrongAnswers: A way to rotate cluster certificates automatically
A method for backing up etcd data
A configuration for container resource limits

questions.question: How do you secure a Kubernetes cluster?
questions.answer:
Security best practices following 4C model:

1. Cloud Security:
   - IAM roles and policies
   - Firewall rules
   - Private networking

2. Cluster Security:
   - Enable RBAC
   - Use minimal privileges
   - Enable audit logging
   - Secure etcd

3. Container Security:
   - Scan images for vulnerabilities
   - Use non-root users
   - Read-only root filesystems
   - Drop capabilities

4. Code Security:
   - Secrets management
   - Network Policies
   - TLS for all communications

Additional:
- Regular Kubernetes updates
- Pod Security Standards
- Disable anonymous API access
wrongAnswers: By disabling all API server logging
By running containers as root by default
By using default service accounts for all workloads

questions.question: What things can increase Kubernetes security?
questions.answer:
Security measures:

1. Network isolation:
   - Set up Network Policies
   - Default deny all
   - Explicit allow only

2. Access control:
   - Use RBAC with least privilege
   - Use ServiceAccounts
   - Disable anonymous access

3. Namespace isolation:
   - Use namespaces for multi-tenancy
   - Set resource quotas

4. Pod security:
   - Avoid privileged containers
   - Use non-root users
   - Read-only root filesystem

5. Secrets:
   - Enable encryption at rest
   - Use external secrets managers
   - Rotate regularly

6. Monitoring:
   - Turn on audit logging
   - Monitor for anomalies
wrongAnswers: Disabling API server authentication entirely
Exposing all namespaces without restrictions
Running containers with host PID namespace

questions.question: How do you manage secrets securely in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
Secret management options:

1. Basic (not recommended):
   - Kubernetes Secrets (base64, not encrypted)
   - Enable encryption at rest for etcd

2. Better:
   - External secrets managers
   - AWS Secrets Manager + External Secrets Operator
   - HashiCorp Vault + Vault Agent
   - GCP Secret Manager

3. Best:
   - CSI drivers for secrets
   - Encryption at rest enabled
   - RBAC for secret access
   - Regular rotation

Enable encryption at rest:
kube-apiserver --encryption-provider-config=/path/to/config

Best practice: Use external secrets management for production.
wrongAnswers: Store secrets in ConfigMaps as plain text
Commit secrets to version control repositories
Use Kubernetes Secrets without enabling etcd encryption

questions.question: What is a Helm chart and how is it used?
questions.answer:
Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes that uses charts.

Charts:
- Collection of files describing Kubernetes resources
- Versioned and shareable
- Deploy complex applications easily

Directory structure:
my-chart/
  Chart.yaml
  values.yaml
  templates/
    deployment.yaml
    service.yaml

Commands:
helm install my-release my-chart
helm upgrade my-release my-chart
helm rollback my-release 1
helm list
helm uninstall my-release

Benefits:
- Reproducible deployments
- Version control for configs
- Templating
- Dependency management
wrongAnswers: A tool for monitoring cluster metrics
A container runtime interface implementation
A networking plugin for pod communication

questions.question: What are Kubernetes Operators?
questions.answer:
Operator = Custom Controller + Custom Resources

Pattern:
- Extends Kubernetes API with custom resources
- Controller watches custom resources
- Automates complex operations

Components:
1. Custom Resource Definition (CRD):
   - Defines new API resource type
   - Example: MyDatabase

2. Custom Controller:
   - Watches for changes
   - Takes action to maintain desired state

Use cases:
- Database management (backup, restore)
- Application lifecycle automation
- Custom compliance requirements

Popular Operators:
- Prometheus Operator
- cert-manager
- Argo CD
- Rook (Ceph storage)
- Elasticsearch Operator
wrongAnswers: A command-line tool for kubectl operations
A GUI dashboard for cluster management
A container image scanning tool

questions.question: What is the purpose of Operators?
questions.answer:
Operators automate complex application management that requires domain-specific knowledge.

Benefits:
- Automates operational knowledge
- Consistent deployments
- Self-healing for custom apps
- Handles upgrades and backups

Without operators:
- Stateful apps need manual management
- Upgrades require human intervention
- Backup/restore is manual

With operators:
- Automated lifecycle management
- Custom reconciliation loops
- Domain-specific logic encoded
- Consistent behavior across clusters
wrongAnswers: To schedule pods on specific nodes
To manage container image repositories
To provide DNS resolution for services

questions.question: What are Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)?
questions.answer:
CRDs extend Kubernetes API with custom resources.

Process:
1. Create CRD definition
2. API server serves new endpoints
3. Create custom resources
4. Controller watches and acts

Example CRD:
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: databases.example.com
spec:
  group: example.com
  names:
    kind: Database
    plural: databases
  scope: Namespaced
  versions:
  - name: v1
    served: true
    storage: true

After creation:
kubectl get databases
wrongAnswers: A way to define custom container images
A method for creating persistent volumes
A configuration for node resource allocation

questions.question: What is a Headless Service and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
Headless Service: ClusterIP set to None

Behavior:
- No cluster IP assigned
- DNS returns Pod IPs directly
- Pods individually addressable

Use cases:
- StatefulSet applications
- Database clusters (MongoDB, Cassandra)
- When client needs to discover all Pod IPs
- Distributed systems requiring direct Pod access

Example:
spec:
  clusterIP: None  # Headless
  selector:
    app: database

DNS resolution:
- Regular Service: <svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local → ClusterIP
- Headless: <pod>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local → PodIP
wrongAnswers: A service without any endpoints configured
A service that exposes pods directly to the internet
A service with zero replicas running

questions.question: How do you upgrade a Kubernetes cluster?
questions.answer:
Standard upgrade procedure:

1. Backup etcd:
   kubectl get etcd-snapshot -o yaml > backup.yaml

2. Upgrade control plane:
   - kube-apiserver
   - kube-controller-manager
   - kube-scheduler
   - etcd

3. Upgrade kubelet on control plane nodes

4. Upgrade worker nodes one by one:
   - Cordon node
   - Drain node (evict Pods)
   - Upgrade kubelet
   - Uncordon node

5. Upgrade add-ons:
   - CNI plugin
   - CoreDNS
   - Ingress controllers

Best practice: Test in staging first!
wrongAnswers: Delete the cluster and create a new one with the new version
Restart all nodes simultaneously
Upgrade worker nodes before the control plane

questions.question: How do you backup and restore a Kubernetes cluster?
questions.answer:
Backup strategies:

1. etcd backup (cluster state):
   ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save backup.db

2. Resources backup:
   kubectl get all -A -o yaml > resources.yaml

3. PersistentVolumes:
   - Cloud provider snapshots
   - Velero for CSI snapshots

4. Full backup with Velero:
   velero backup create backup-001
   velero backup get

Restore:
   velero restore create restore-001 --from-backup backup-001

Best practices:
- Regular automated backups
- Test restoration periodically
- Store backups off-site
- Document restoration procedure
wrongAnswers: Use kubectl cp to copy all YAML files
Backing up is automatic and not needed manually
Export logs to a file as the primary backup method

questions.question: How do you backup and restore etcd?
questions.answer:
etcd backup:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /tmp/etcd-backup.db \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

etcd restore:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /tmp/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd/restore

Restore process:
1. Stop API server
2. Restore etcd data
3. Restart etcd
4. Verify cluster state
5. Restart control plane components
wrongAnswers: Use kubectl exec to copy files from etcd pods
etcd data is automatically replicated and never needs backing up
Restart the kube-apiserver to create a backup

questions.question: What are admission controllers?
questions.answer:
Admission controllers intercept API requests after authentication but before persistence.


Built-in controllers:
- NamespaceLifecycle: Prevent deletion of system namespaces
- LimitRanger: Enforce resource limits
- ServiceAccount: Auto-mount ServiceAccount tokens
- PersistentVolumeLabel: Add cloud labels to PVs
- DefaultStorageClass: Set default StorageClass
- PodSecurity: Enforce pod security standards

Types:
1. Mutating: Modify request objects
2. Validating: Accept or reject requests

Order matters: Mutating runs first

Enable custom admission:
- ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
- MutatingWebhookConfiguration
wrongAnswers: Controllers that admit nodes to the cluster
A type of load balancer for the API server
Authentication mechanisms for users logging in

questions.question: What is a mutating admission webhook?
questions.answer:
A mutating admission webhook modifies Kubernetes objects before they are persisted.


Process:
1. API request received
2. Authentication and authorization
3. Mutating admission webhooks run (can modify objects)
4. Validating admission webhooks run
5. Object persisted to etcd

Common uses:
- Injecting sidecars (Istio)
- Setting default values for Pods
- Enforcing best practices (resource limits)
- Adding security configurations
- Auto-adding labels/annotations

Example: Automatically add resource limits to Deployments without them.
wrongAnswers: A webhook that mutates container images to the latest version
A tool for mutating YAML files before applying them
A validating webhook that rejects malformed resources

questions.question: What is a service mesh and why use it?
questions.answer:
A service mesh manages service-to-service communication in Kubernetes.

Features:
- Traffic management: Load balancing, canary deployments, A/B testing
- Security: mTLS encryption between services
- Observability: Tracing, metrics, logging

Benefits:
- No code changes required
- Consistent across all services
- Centralized policy enforcement

Popular service meshes:
- Istio: Full-featured, complex
- Linkerd: Simple, lightweight
- Consul Connect: HashiCorp ecosystem
- Cilium: eBPF-based

Trade-offs:
- Adds complexity
- Resource overhead
- Requires expertise
wrongAnswers: A mesh network for connecting multiple clusters
A database caching layer for services
A message broker for asynchronous communication

questions.question: How do you implement high availability in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
HA implementation:

1. Control Plane HA:
   - Multiple API servers
   - etcd cluster (3 or 5 nodes)
 - Multiple controller managers (leader election)
   - Multiple schedulers (leader election)

2. Worker Node HA:
   - Multiple worker nodes
   - Pod anti-affinity for spreading
   - PodDisruptionBudgets
   - Replicas for Deployments

3. Application HA:
   - Multiple replicas
   - Readiness probes
   - Horizontal scaling (HPA)
   - Pod anti-affinity

4. Data HA:
   - StatefulSets with proper storage
   - Replication across zones
   - Regular backups
wrongAnswers: Running a single etcd instance for simplicity
Disabling pod disruption budgets to allow faster failover
Using the default scheduler without any configuration

questions.question: How do you implement Kubernetes multi-tenancy?
questions.answer:
Multi-tenancy allows multiple teams/applications to share a cluster securely.


Soft Multi-tenancy:
- Namespaces for isolation
- RBAC for access control
- NetworkPolicies to restrict traffic
- ResourceQuotas for resource limits
- LimitRanges for defaults

Hard Multi-tenancy:
- Virtual clusters (vcluster)
- Separate control planes per tenant
- Node isolation with node selectors

Best practices:
- Always use RBAC
- Default deny NetworkPolicies
- ResourceQuotas per namespace
- Regular auditing
- Tenant isolation review
wrongAnswers: Allow all users to be cluster admins by default
Disable namespaces and use the default namespace only
Remove all RBAC policies for easier access

questions.question: How do you manage resource quotas in multi-tenant environments?
questions.answer:
ResourceQuotas prevent namespace over-consumption:

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: team-quota
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "20"

LimitRanges set container defaults:
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: defaults
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    default:
      memory: 512Mi
      cpu: 500m

Commands:
kubectl describe resourcequota -n <namespace>
wrongAnswers: Set quotas only at the cluster level, not namespace level
Remove all quotas to maximize resource utilization
Use node selectors instead of ResourceQuotas

questions.question: What is GKE (Google Kubernetes Engine)?
questions.answer:
GKE is Google's managed Kubernetes service on Google Cloud Platform.

Features:
- Managed control plane
- Auto-upgrades for Kubernetes version
- Auto-repair for failed nodes
- Node auto-provisioning
- Horizontal and vertical Pod autoscaling
- Integrated networking (VPC)
- Cloud SQL for managed databases

Benefits:
- No need to manage control plane
- Integrated with GCP services
- Built-in security features
- SLA for availability

Cost considerations:
- Pay for node instances
- Control plane is free (standard)
- Premium features cost extra
wrongAnswers: A Kubernetes distribution for on-premises data centers
A container registry service from Google
A managed database service using Kubernetes

questions.question: How do you run Kubernetes locally?
questions.answer:
Options for local Kubernetes:

1. Minikube (recommended for beginners):
   minikube start
   minikube dashboard
   minikube stop

2. kind (Kubernetes in Docker):
   kind create cluster
   kind delete cluster

3. k3s (Lightweight K8s):
   curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh -

4. Docker Desktop:
   - Enable Kubernetes in settings
   - Uses WSL2 on Windows

5. k0s, MicroK8s, EKS-D

Minikube features:
- Single-node cluster
- Multiple addons
- Easy to start/stop
- Good for learning
wrongAnswers: Docker Swarm provides local Kubernetes
Kubernetes cannot run locally
Use AWS EKS local profile for local development

questions.question: What is the role of Load Balancer in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
Load Balancer distributes incoming traffic across Pods.

In Kubernetes:
- External Load Balancer auto-provisioned by cloud provider
- Routes traffic to Service endpoints
- Provides single IP for external access

How it works:
1. Service type: LoadBalancer
2. Cloud provider creates LB
3. LB health checks Pods
4. Traffic routed to healthy Pods
5- Algorithm: Round-robin (default)

Benefits:
- High availability
- Single entry point
- Automatic failover
- SSL termination

Limitations:
- One LB per Service (can be expensive)
- Cloud provider dependent
wrongAnswers: A tool for balancing cluster resource usage
A component for scheduling pods across nodes
An internal DNS service for service discovery

questions.question: What is Kubernetes Load Balancing?
questions.answer:
Kubernetes provides two types of load balancing:

1. Internal Load Balancer:
   - Automatically balances loads
   - Allocates Pods with incoming load
   - Used for internal traffic

2. External Load Balancer:
   - Directs traffic from external loads
   - Distributes to backend Pods
   - Used for public traffic

Implementation:
- kube-proxy programs iptables/IPVS
- Service provides ClusterIP
- Endpoints are updated automatically
- Cloud providers provision external LB
wrongAnswers: A mechanism for balancing CPU across cluster nodes
A feature for distributing container images
A method for managing storage allocations

questions.question: What are different ways to provide external network connectivity to K8s?
questions.answer:
Options for external access:

1. NodePort:
   - Exposes on static port (30000-32767) on all nodes
   - Simple but less secure

2. LoadBalancer:
   - Cloud provider provisions external LB
   - Best for production

3. Ingress:
   - L7 HTTP/HTTPS routing
   - URL-based routing
   - SSL termination

4. Kube-proxy with port forward:
   kubectl proxy --port=8080
   http://localhost:8080/api/v1/proxy/namespaces/<ns>/services/<svc>:/

5. ExternalName Service:
   - Maps to external DNS name
   - For accessing external services
wrongAnswers: Using ConfigMaps to expose network configuration
Configuring DNS records directly in etcd
Using Kubernetes Secrets for network credentials

questions.question: What is the garbage collection mechanism in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
Kubernetes automatically cleans up unused resources.

Trigger types:

1. Owner references:
   - When Deployment creates ReplicaSet
   - ReplicaSet has owner reference to Deployment
   - If Deployment deleted, ReplicaSet garbage collected

2. TTL controllers:
   - Jobs after completion
   - ttlSecondsAfterFinished

3. Namespace deletion:
   - Cascade delete all resources

4. Orphaned resources:
   - No owner reference
   - Must be manually deleted

5. Completed/Failed Pods:
   - Based on ttlSecondsAfterFinished or policy
   - Successful pods may be kept for debugging
wrongAnswers: A feature for cleaning up unused Docker images on nodes
Manual deletion of resources using kubectl
A background process that deletes failed Jobs only

questions.question: How do you secure container images?
questions.answer:
Image security best practices:

1. Use minimal base images:
   - alpine, distroless, scratch
   - Fewer vulnerabilities

2. Scan images:
   - Trivy, Clair, Snyk
   - Integrate in CI/CD

3. Sign images:
   - Cosign, Notary
   - Verify before deployment

4. Private registries:
   - Use imagePullSecrets
   kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
     --docker-server=... --docker-username=... --docker-password=...


5. Security context:
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
    capabilities:
      drop: ["ALL"]
wrongAnswers: Use the latest tag for all container images
Store images in public repositories without scanning
Disable container security contexts for performance

questions.question: What are the key kubectl commands for debugging?
questions.answer:
Essential debugging commands:

# Cluster info
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
kubectl api-resources

# Pod management
kubectl get pods -o wide
kubectl describe pod <name>
kubectl logs <pod-name>
kubectl logs <pod> --previous

# Execution
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh
kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /path/file

# Networking
kubectl port-forward pod/<pod> 8080:80
kubectl top pods
kubectl get endpoints <svc>

# All namespaces
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl describe pod <pod> -n <namespace>

# Events
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
wrongAnswers: kubectl delete pod to restart it for fresh logs
kubectl edit pod to fix configuration issues
kubectl run for all debugging tasks

questions.question: What is the difference between etcd and Kubernetes API server?
questions.answer:
etcd:
- Distributed key-value store
- Stores all cluster data persistently
- Single source of truth
- Handles data persistence
- Backup and restore focus

API Server (kube-apiserver):
- REST API interface
- Handles all requests
- Authentication and authorization
- Validation of requests
- Updates etcd based on requests

Relationship:
Client → API Server → etcd

API Server reads/writes to etcd.
All components talk to API Server, not directly to etcd.
wrongAnswers: etcd is the API server for external clients
Both store container images
etcd handles authentication while API server handles storage

questions.question: What are Kubernetes API groups?
questions.answer:
API groups organize Kubernetes resources.

Core API Group (no prefix):
- /api/v1
- Pods, Services, Nodes, Namespaces, etc.

Named API Groups:
- /apis/batch/v1 - Jobs, CronJobs
- /apis/apps/v1 - Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets
- /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1 - NetworkPolicies, Ingresses
- /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 - Roles, RoleBindings
- /apis/autoscaling/v2 - HorizontalPodAutoscalers
- /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1 - StorageClasses

API discovery:
kubectl api-resources
kubectl api-versions
wrongAnswers: Groups for organizing different Kubernetes clusters
A way to group users by their access levels
Container image registry namespaces

questions.question: What is Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)?
questions.answer:
VPA automatically adjusts Pod resource requests.

Modes:
- Off: No automatic adjustment
- Initial: Set on Pod creation only
- Auto: Update and evict (restart) Pods
- Recreation: Recreate Pods with new limits

Benefits:
- Optimizes resource allocation
- Reduces waste
- Auto-corrects under-provisioning

Note: VPA and HPA can work together:
- HPA scales horizontally (more Pods)
- VPA scales vertically (more resources per Pod)

Limitation: Cannot have HPA and VPA on same metric (CPU/memory) for same Deployment.
wrongAnswers: A tool for auto-scaling the number of nodes in the cluster
A feature for automatically deleting pending pods
A mechanism for vertical pod distribution across zones

questions.question: What is Cluster Autoscaler?
questions.answer:
Cluster Autoscaler adjusts the number of worker nodes in a cluster.

How it works:
1. Monitors Pod scheduling status
2. If Pods pending due to insufficient resources
3. Scales up node pool (provisions new nodes)
4. Waits for nodes to join
5. Schedules Pods

Scale down:
1. Monitors node utilization
2. If node under-utilized for period
3. Evicts Pods (respecting PDBs)
4. Terminates node

Requirements:
- Cloud provider support (GKE, EKS, AKS)
- Node pools configured
- Proper node labels/selectors
wrongAnswers: A tool for auto-scaling pods within a deployment
A component that automatically scales container images
A feature for scaling persistent volume storage

questions.question: How does Kubernetes storage orchestration work?
questions.answer:
Storage orchestration uses CSI (Container Storage Interface).

Key Components:
1. PersistentVolume (PV):
   - Cluster-wide storage resource
   - Admin provisioned or dynamic

2. PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC):
   - Request for storage by user
   - Claims specific size and access mode

3. StorageClass:
   - Defines type of storage (SSD, HDD, encrypted)
   - Links to CSI driver
   - Enables dynamic provisioning

Lifecycle:
1. Admin creates StorageClass
2. User creates PVC
3. StorageClass dynamically provisions PV
4. PVC bound to PV
5. Pod mounts PVC

CSI enables vendor-neutral storage plugins.
wrongAnswers: Using ConfigMaps for persistent storage
Storing data directly in etcd for all volumes
Manual SSH to nodes to create directories

questions.question: What is a DaemonSet and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
DaemonSet ensures a copy of a Pod runs on all (or selected) nodes.

Use cases:
- Log collectors (Fluentd, Filebeat)
- Monitoring agents (Prometheus node exporter)
- Storage daemons (Ceph, GlusterFS)
- Network plugins (CNI)
- kube-proxy

How it works:
- New nodes added → Pod automatically deployed
- Node removed → Pod garbage collected
- No need for scheduling decisions

Example:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: logging-agent
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: fluentd
        image: fluentd
wrongAnswers: A Deployment that creates daemon processes
A Job that runs pods on all nodes once
A Service that exposes daemons to the network

questions.question: What is the difference between DaemonSet and ReplicaSet?
questions.answer:
ReplicaSet:
- Ensures specified number of Pods run
- Scheduler decides which nodes
- May not run on all nodes
- Good for stateless applications

DaemonSet:
- One Pod per node (or subset)
- Automatically deployed to new nodes
- Cannot use scheduling priorities
- Good for cluster-wide agents

Example:
- ReplicaSet: 3 web server Pods across any nodes
- DaemonSet: Logging agent on every node
wrongAnswers: DaemonSet creates more replicas than specified
ReplicaSet runs on all nodes while DaemonSet does not
They are identical resource types with different names

questions.question: How do you troubleshoot if Pod is not getting scheduled?
questions.answer:
Debug steps for unscheduled Pods:

1. Check Pod status:
   kubectl describe pod <pod-name>
   Look for Events section

2. Common causes:
   - Insufficient resources (CPU/memory)
   - Node selectors not matching
   - Taints without tolerations
   - PVC not bound
   - Node disk pressure/memory pressure

3. Check node resources:
   kubectl describe node <node-name>
   kubectl top nodes

4. Check PVC status:
   kubectl get pvc
   kubectl describe pvc <name>

5. Check for taints:
   kubectl describe nodes | grep Taints
wrongAnswers: Delete the pod and recreate it
Increase the pod replica count
Restart the Kubernetes API server

questions.question: Debugging a slow Kubernetes application
questions.answer:
Steps to debug slow application:

1. Check Pod resource utilization:
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory

2. Describe slow Pod:
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>
Look for resource throttling, restarts

3. Check container logs:
kubectl logs <pod-name>
Look for timeouts, errors

4. Test network latency:
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- ping <target>
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- curl http://<service>

5. Verify node health:
kubectl describe node <node-name>

6. Check for throttling:
- CPU throttling indicators
- Memory pressure
wrongAnswers: Scale up the deployment to more replicas
Use kubectl run to create a faster pod
Restart the container runtime on all nodes

questions.question: Cluster resources exhausted, Pods in Pending state
questions.answer:
Debugging '0/X nodes available: insufficient CPU and memory':

1. Check cluster resource availability:
kubectl describe node <node-name>
kubectl top nodes

2. Check which Pods consume most:
kubectl top pods --all-namespaces

3. Scale down non-essential:
kubectl scale deployment <name> --replicas=0

4. Add more nodes:
- Cloud console or kubectl
- Or use cluster autoscaler

5. Set resource quotas:
- LimitRange for defaults
- ResourceQuota per namespace

6. Optimize existing workloads:
- Review resource requests/limits
- Remove unused deployments
wrongAnswers: Delete all pending pods to free resources
Increase the pod restart policy
Use a larger container image size

questions.question: How to forward ports in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
Port forwarding commands:


Pod port forwarding:
kubectl port-forward pod/<pod-name> 8080:80

Service port forwarding:
kubectl port-forward svc/<service-name> 8080:80

Node port forwarding (requires direct node access):
SSH to node, then:
socat TCP-LISTEN:8080,fork TCP:<pod-ip>:80

Using kubectl proxy:
kubectl proxy --port=8080
Then access via:
http://localhost:8080/api/v1/namespaces/<ns>/pods/<pod-name>/proxy/

Use cases:
- Debugging locally
- Accessing databases
- Testing services
wrongAnswers: Configure NodePort service for all debugging
Use SSH tunneling to reach pods directly
Edit the pod spec to expose ports externally

questions.question: What are the Kubernetes control plane components?
questions.answer:
Control Plane Components:

1. kube-apiserver
   - Frontend for Kubernetes API
   - Authenticates, authorizes, validates
   - All operations go through it

2. etcd
   - Distributed key-value store
   - Stores all cluster state
   - Should be HA for production

3. kube-scheduler
   - Assigns Pods to nodes
   - Considers resources, constraints

4. kube-controller-manager
   - Node controller
   - Replication controller
   - Endpoints controller
   - Service account controller

5. cloud-controller-manager (cloud K8s)
   - Interfaces with cloud APIs
wrongAnswers: kubelet and kube-proxy
Container runtime only
kubectl and helm

questions.question: What is the difference between StatefulSet and Deployment?
questions.answer:
Deployment:
- Stateless applications
- Pods are interchangeable
- Rolling updates, rollback support
- Single DNS name for all Pods
- Any Pod can handle requests

StatefulSet:
- Stateful applications
- Stable, unique network identifiers (pod-0, pod-1)
- Stable persistent storage
- Ordered deployment, scaling, deletion
- Each Pod has persistent DNS

Use Deployment for:
- Web servers, APIs
- Microservices

Use StatefulSet for:
- Databases
- Message queues
- Any app needing stable identity
wrongAnswers: StatefulSet cannot use persistent storage
Deployment does not support rolling updates
They are the same with different names

questions.question: What is the Kubernetes garbage collection?
questions.answer:
Kubernetes automatically cleans up unused resources through garbage collection.


Mechanisms:
1. Owner references:
   - Parent-child relationships
   - When parent deleted, children cleaned up
   - Example: ReplicaSet owns Pods

2. TTL controllers:
   - Jobs can have ttlSecondsAfterFinished
   - Pods cleaned after TTL expires

3. Orphaned resources:
   - Resources without owners
   - Must be manually deleted or by custom controllers

4. Completed Pods:
   - Based on TTL or when Job completes

5. Failed containers:
   - Restarts tracked by kubelet
   - CrashLoopBackOff has limits
wrongAnswers: A tool for cleaning up unused Docker images
Manual cleanup using kubectl prune
Automatic deletion of all completed Jobs

questions.question: How do you encrypt Kubernetes secrets in etcd?
questions.answer:
Enable encryption at rest for etcd:

1. Create encryption config:
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
    - secrets
    providers:
    - aescbc:
        keys:
        - name: key1
          secret: <BASE64_ENCODED_KEY>
    - identity: {}

2. Add to kube-apiserver:
command:
  - kube-apiserver
  - --encryption-provider-config=/path/to/config.yaml

3. Restart API server

4. Verify:
kubectl get secret <name> -o yaml
Should show encrypted data
wrongAnswers: Secrets are encrypted by default in Kubernetes
Use kubectl encrypt to encrypt existing secrets
Configure encryption in the container runtime

questions.question: What is Pod Security Standards (PSS)?
questions.answer:
Pod Security Standards define security levels for Pods.


Three policy levels:

1. privileged (unrestricted)
   - Full access to host
   - For infrastructure Pods

2. baseline (minimally restricted)
   - Prevents known privilege escalations
   - Default for many workloads

3. restricted (fully restricted)
   - Enforces best practices
   - For security-sensitive workloads

Enforcement via:
- Pod security admission (built-in)
- Pod security policies (deprecated)

Namespace labels:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: production
  labels:
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
wrongAnswers: A standard for pod naming conventions
A requirement for all pods to run as root
A networking configuration for pod communication

questions.question: What is Kubernetes Events and how to use them for debugging?
questions.answer:
Kubernetes Events track cluster and resource changes.

View events:
kubectl get events
kubectl get events -n <namespace>
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

Watch events:
kubectl get events -w

Event fields:
- TYPE: Normal, Warning
- REASON: Why event occurred
- OBJECT: Affected resource
- MESSAGE: Human-readable description

Common reasons:
- Scheduled, Pulled, Created, Started
- PullBackOff, Failed, BackOff
- NodeNotReady, NetworkNotReady

Events are short-lived (~1 hour).
For persistence, use logging or observability tools.
wrongAnswers: Logs stored in container images
Metrics collected by Prometheus only
Persistent records stored in etcd forever

questions.question: How do you implement blue-green deployment in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
Blue-green deployment strategy:

1. Deploy green version alongside blue:
kubectl apply -f green-deployment.yaml

2. Services initially point to blue

3. Create/update Service to point to green:
kubectl patch service my-service \
  -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"my-app","version":"green"}}}'


4. Monitor green deployment

5. If issues, rollback by pointing back to blue

6. Clean up old (blue) when confident

Benefits:
- Instant switchover
- Easy rollback
- Zero downtime

Considerations:
- Double resource usage during transition
- Database migrations need careful handling
- Session persistence may be affected
wrongAnswers: Delete the old version before deploying the new one
Use a single Deployment with multiple replicas
Automatically merge both versions into one

questions.question: How do you implement canary deployment in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
Canary deployment releases to subset of users:


Method 1: Two Deployments
- Canary deployment (e.g., 10% replicas)
- Stable deployment (e.g., 90% replicas)

Method 2: Service mesh (Istio)
- Configure traffic splitting
- 10% to new version, 90% to old

Example with weight-based routing:
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
kind: VirtualService
spec:
  http:
  - route:
    - destination:
        host: my-service
        subset: stable
      weight: 90
    - destination:
        host: my-service
        subset: canary
      weight: 10

Steps:
1. Deploy canary with fewer replicas
2. Route small % traffic to canary
3. Monitor metrics
4. Gradually increase traffic
5. Complete or rollback
wrongAnswers: Deploy canary to all users first
Use canary only for database migrations
Replace the stable version entirely with canary

questions.question: What is Kubernetes Service Topology?
questions.answer:
Service Topology routes traffic based on source/destination.

Feature: service.kubernetes.io/topology-mode

Options:
- Any: Any node (default)
- Close: Same node preferred
- Closer: Same node > same zone > same region
- Heterogeneous: Cross-zone allowed

Use cases:
- Reduce network costs (prefer same zone)
- Lower latency (prefer same node)
- High availability (spread across zones)

Example annotation:
service.kubernetes.io/topology-mode: Closer

Works with kube-proxy in iptables/IPVS mode.
wrongAnswers: A way to configure pod network topology
A method for organizing services into clusters
A DNS configuration for service discovery

questions.question: What is the lifecycle of a Pod?
questions.answer:
Pod lifecycle states:

1. Pending:
   - Pod accepted by cluster
   - Container images being pulled or containers starting

2. Running:
   - Pod bound to node
   - At least one container running

3. Succeeded:
   - All containers terminated successfully
   - Will not be restarted

4. Failed:
   - All containers terminated
   - At least one container failed with non-zero status

5. Unknown:
   - Cannot communicate with Pod

Additional states:
- CrashLoopBackOff: Container failing repeatedly
- ImagePullBackOff: Cannot pull image
- Init: Init containers running
wrongAnswers: Pod lifecycle is only about container restarts
Pods go directly from Pending to Terminating
Running is the final state for all pods

questions.question: What are the different ways to configure environment variables?
questions.answer:
Environment variables in Pods:

1. Static values:
env:
- name: APP_ENV
  value: "production"

2. From ConfigMap:
env:
- name: CONFIG_VAR
  valueFrom:
    configMapKeyRef:
      name: my-config
      key: some-key

3. From Secret:
env:
- name: DB_PASSWORD
  valueFrom:
    secretKeyRef:
      name: db-secrets
      key: password

4. From field reference:
env:
- name: POD_NAME
  valueFrom:
    fieldRef:
      fieldPath: metadata.name

5. From resource:
env:
- name: CPU_REQUEST
  valueFrom:
    resourceFieldRef:
      containerName: app
      resource: requests.cpu
wrongAnswers: Only through command-line arguments in the container spec
Using persistent volumes for all environment configuration
Hardcoding values directly in container images

questions.question: How do you debug network issues in Kubernetes?
questions.answer:
Network debugging steps:

1. Verify Service exists:
   kubectl get svc

2. Check endpoint slices:
   kubectl get endpoints <service-name>

3. Test DNS resolution:
   kubectl exec -it <pod> -- nslookup <service>

4. Test connectivity:
   kubectl exec -it <pod> -- curl <service>:<port>

5. Test pod-to-pod:
   kubectl exec -it <pod> -- ping <target-pod-ip>

6. Check NetworkPolicy:
   kubectl get networkpolicies
   kubectl describe networkpolicy <name>

7. Verify CNI status:
   kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep cni

8. Check kube-proxy:
   kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep kube-proxy
wrongAnswers: Restart the container runtime on all nodes
Delete and recreate all affected pods
Check the cluster etcd backup status

questions.question: What is the Kubernetes node component kubelet?
questions.answer:
Kubelet is an agent running on each node in the cluster.

Responsibilities:
- Registers node with cluster
- Watches for Pod assignments via API server
- Mounts volumes and secrets
- Runs container health checks (probes)
- Reports node and Pod status to API server
- Ensures containers are running as specified

What it does NOT do:
- Manage containers directly (delegates to container runtime)
- Create containers (only instructs runtime)

Key files:
- /var/lib/kubelet: State and config
- /etc/kubernetes/manifests: Static pods

Kubelet configuration:
- Via kubelet config file
- Via command-line flags
wrongAnswers: A control plane component for scheduling pods
A tool for creating and managing persistent volumes
An API server for external client access

questions.question: What is the Container Storage Interface (CSI)?
questions.answer:
CSI is the standard for container storage orchestration.

Purpose:
- Vendor-neutral plugin interface
- Allows third-party storage providers
- Replaces in-tree volume plugins

Benefits:
- Storage vendors can release plugins without K8s core changes
- Easier to develop and maintain
- Consistent across Kubernetes, Mesos, Cloud Foundry

Components:
1. CSI Driver: Implemented by storage vendor
2. CSI Provisioner: Creates/deletes volumes
3. CSI Node Plugin: Mounts volumes to Pods

StorageClass example:
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-storage
provisioner: csi-driver.example.com
parameters:
  type: ssd
wrongAnswers: A container networking interface for pod communication
A tool for managing container images
An API for managing Kubernetes control plane components
